subject: Bret Victor

mental_models.name: Immediate Connection (Creator-Creation Coupling)
mental_models.description: Creators need an immediate connection to what they're creating. When a programmer writes code and must wait for a compile-run-observe cycle, the gap between cause and effect breaks the cognitive loop that enables creativity. The medium must close this gap to near-zero. In music, a pianist hears every note as they play it. In programming, the equivalent would be seeing every behavior change as you type.
mental_models.canonical_example: A game level editor where moving a slider instantly moves the character on screen — the creator experiences the behavior as directly as a sculptor feels clay. Contrast: editing a text file, running compile, watching a separate output — creator is disconnected from creation.
mental_models.implication: Any tool that introduces latency between action and effect breaks the creative loop. The design question for any programming tool is: how small is the gap?

mental_models.name: Representations Are Thought
mental_models.source: Media for Thinking the Unthinkable, 2013; Kill Math, 2011
mental_models.description: The representation you use to express an idea determines what thoughts about that idea are possible. Symbolic math makes some thoughts easy and others hard — not because of mathematics, but because of the notation. Text-based code makes some program structures easy and others hard — not because of computation, but because of the representation. New representations don't just express existing thoughts more clearly — they make new thoughts possible.
mental_models.quote: Our representations of a system are how we understand it.
mental_models.historical_example: Richard Hamming: some thoughts are literally unthinkable without the right notation. Writing enabled history. Mathematical notation enabled physics. New computational media could enable thoughts currently unthinkable.
mental_models.implication: When you design an interface, you are designing what thoughts are possible for the person using it.

mental_models.name: The Medium Constrains the Message
mental_models.source: Multiple essays; Media for Thinking the Unthinkable, 2013
mental_models.description: Current software is trapped in representations designed for physical media. Text-based programming was designed for line printers, not for human understanding. Symbolic mathematics was designed for pencil and paper. Spreadsheets are glorified ledgers. These are not fundamental truths about computation — they are historical accidents that we inherited and forgot to question.
mental_models.quote: Programming languages are written languages — they were designed for writing.
mental_models.application: Before accepting any design convention, ask: what physical constraint originally produced this convention? Is that constraint still relevant?

mental_models.name: See the Data (Visibility Principle)
mental_models.description: A programmer — or any tool user — can only understand what they can see. Hidden state is the fundamental source of programming bugs, learning failures, and system mysteries. The design imperative: make everything visible, especially data, flow, and state that currently live only in the programmer's head.
quotes: People understand what they can see. If a programmer cannot see what a program is doing, she can't understand it.
The entire purpose of code is to manipulate data, and we never see the data. We write with blindfolds, and we read by playing pretend with data-phantoms.
Spreadsheets rule because they show the data.
mental_models.implication: The test of any programming tool: is the data visible? If you have to execute the program mentally to understand what it's doing, the tool has failed.

mental_models.name: Active Reading (Explorable Explanations)
mental_models.description: Reading is currently passive — the reader receives fixed text. But understanding is active — it requires manipulation, experimentation, and direct engagement with the subject matter. Documents should be reactive: readers should be able to change parameters, see consequences, and explore the space of the idea rather than just the single path the author chose to narrate.
mental_models.canonical_example: A description of population dynamics should include a live model where the reader can change birth rate and death rate and watch the population curve change in real time — not just a static equation and a graph.
mental_models.implication: Every 'explanatory document' that isn't interactive is leaving most of its explanatory potential on the table.

mental_models.name: Interactivity as Last Resort
mental_models.description: For information software — software whose primary job is to help people understand something — interaction is a concession, not a feature. When software makes users interact to get information, it requires them to know what they want before asking, learn a navigation language, and remember state across time. Software should infer context from the environment and history first; resort to interaction only when neither suffices.
context_priority: 1. Environment (current time, location, open documents, recent files) — infer from this first
2. History (past behavior patterns, preferences) — infer from this second
3. Interaction (user input) — use only when 1 and 2 are insufficient
mental_models.quote_paraphrase: Interactivity is a curse for users and a crutch for designers.
mental_models.implication: Designing an interface with lots of buttons and menus is not designing for the user — it's offloading design work onto the user. A great information tool eliminates most navigation.

mental_models.name: The Ladder of Abstraction
mental_models.source: Up and Down the Ladder of Abstraction, 2011
mental_models.description: Understanding any system requires the ability to move fluidly between levels of abstraction — from specific concrete instances up to general patterns, and back down to test whether the pattern holds. Tools that lock you at one level of abstraction prevent genuine understanding. A thinker must be able to see a specific behavior (one running instance) and the general pattern (many instances, parameter space) simultaneously.
mental_models.application: When designing or debugging any system: zoom out to see the space of all behaviors, then zoom in to examine specific cases. Never trust a generalization you can't instantiate. Never trust a specific example you can't generalize.
mental_models.implication: Programming tools that show only one execution at a time are fundamentally limited — you cannot understand a program from a single run.

mental_models.name: Living Behavior vs. Dead Appearance
mental_models.description: The distinction between tools that simulate static appearance vs. tools that express living behavior. A tool for animators that lets them draw a fish — frame by frame — produces a 'dead fish': an image that simulates life. A tool that lets animators define how the fish reacts, moves in response to stimuli, expresses intent — produces living behavior. The gap is between procedural expression and static representation.
mental_models.implication: For any creative tool: is the creator expressing behavior, or simulating appearance? The former is alive; the latter is a simulation of life.
mental_models.extension: This applies to all software: a UI that responds to user intent is alive; a UI that displays static state with navigation handles is a dead fish.

mental_models.name: Principle as Moral Obligation
mental_models.description: A principle is not a preference or a style. It is a moral obligation — something that, when violated, produces wrong in the world. Victor's principle ('creators need immediate connection to what they're creating') is not a UX preference — it is a belief that violating it is actively harmful to human creativity and cognition. Historical examples: Larry Tesler's principle ('no modes'), Richard Stallman's ('software must be free'), Doug Engelbart's ('augment human intellect'). Each was a moral commitment that structured an entire career.
mental_models.implication: The question for any serious practitioner is not 'what is my aesthetic?' but 'what is my principle?' A principle you'd be willing to spend decades fighting for — not because it makes products better, but because its opposite is wrong.

mental_models.name: Communal Computing (Dynamicland Model)
mental_models.description: Current computers isolate people — one person, one screen, one private mental model. Dynamicland's thesis: computation should be spatial, shared, and embodied. When computation exists in a room — projected on physical paper, manipulable with hands, visible to everyone — it becomes a communal cognitive tool rather than an individual one. This is how knowledge was shared before computers (whiteboards, maps, blueprints) and how it could be shared again at computational power levels.
mental_models.quote: a computer that is a place, where people work together in the real world
mental_models.implication: The individual-screen model of computing is a historical accident of the personal computer, not a fundamental truth. Computation embedded in shared physical space is a different, possibly superior paradigm for collective understanding.

decision_fingerprints.trigger: Someone proposes a feature with a menu, button, or dialog
decision_fingerprints.response: Ask what information the user needs, not what interaction they should perform. Can the software infer this from context? If yes, the interaction shouldn't exist.

decision_fingerprints.trigger: A design 'just works' but requires mental simulation to understand
decision_fingerprints.response: The design has failed. Make the state visible. A user should never have to imagine what the program is doing.

decision_fingerprints.trigger: Someone justifies a convention with 'that's how it's always been done'
decision_fingerprints.response: Ask what physical or historical constraint produced that convention. If the constraint is no longer relevant, the convention is not required.

decision_fingerprints.trigger: A programming tutorial or education tool teaches syntax
decision_fingerprints.response: Learning syntax is not learning to program. What does the environment show the learner? Can they see the data? Can they see the flow? Can they create by reacting?

decision_fingerprints.trigger: Someone describes an interface as 'interactive' as if that's inherently good
decision_fingerprints.response: Interaction is the last resort. What can be inferred from environment and history? What interaction can be eliminated entirely?

decision_fingerprints.trigger: A tool requires the user to imagine behavior without seeing it
decision_fingerprints.response: The tool is asking the user to be the computer. That's wrong. The computer should be the computer.

heuristics: Show the data — always. If it's hidden, it shouldn't be.
The gap between action and effect is the enemy of creativity.
Interaction is a design failure. What can be inferred?
Every convention has a history. What physical constraint produced it?
A person is not a machine and should not be forced to think like one.
Visualize data, not code. Dynamic behavior, not static structure.
The medium determines what thoughts are possible.
A representation isn't just a display — it's a cognitive tool.
Can you see the space of all behaviors, or just one?
Spreadsheets rule because they show the data. Everything else should too.
